racial.summary: Panama is one of the most ethnically diverse countries in Latin America — mestizo, Black/Afro-Panamanian (15-20%), indigenous (12%), Chinese-Panamanian, South Asian, and white populations coexist. However, a colorism hierarchy exists with lighter skin correlating to socioeconomic status. Afro-Panamanians and indigenous peoples face documented discrimination in employment and housing. American retirees of all races generally receive positive treatment due to economic status, but Black Americans may encounter colorism in social settings.
racial.legalProtections: Constitution Title III Chapter 1 prohibits racial discrimination, Law 16 of 2002 (anti-discrimination), Anti-Discrimination Commission (CONACOD), Law 11 of 2005 (anti-racism)
positiveFactors: Genuinely multiracial society — diversity is part of national identity
Panama Canal Zone history created long tradition of American residents of all races
Interracial relationships and mixed-race families are normalized
CONACOD provides complaint mechanism for discrimination
Economic status often overrides racial bias for retirees
riskFactors: Colorism hierarchy well-documented — lighter skin correlates to economic power
Indigenous communities face systemic marginalization per UN reports
Afro-Panamanians underrepresented in government and private sector leadership
Media representation skews heavily toward lighter-skinned Panamanians
Some gated communities and social clubs have informal racial exclusion patterns

sources.title: US State Department Human Rights Report - Panama 2023
UN CERD Concluding Observations on Panama

religious.summary: Panama has strong religious freedom. While predominantly Catholic (~60%) and increasingly Protestant/Evangelical (~25%), the constitution guarantees freedom of religion and Panama has notable Jewish, Muslim, Hindu, Baha'i, and Buddhist communities. Panama City has one of the oldest Jewish communities in the Americas and a prominent Hindu temple. Religious discrimination is rare and not politically charged.
religious.legalProtections: Constitution Article 35 guarantees freedom of religion, no state religion, religious organizations can register for legal recognition
positiveFactors: Genuine religious pluralism — synagogues, mosques, Hindu temples, Buddhist temples all present in Panama City
Kol Shearith Israel synagogue is one of the oldest in the Americas
No restrictions on religious dress or expression
Religious holidays from multiple traditions are socially recognized
Strong tradition of interfaith business community
riskFactors: Catholic cultural dominance means some social pressure in certain contexts
Evangelical Christian movement has growing political influence on social policy
Non-religious individuals may encounter assumptions about faith
Some indigenous religious practices are marginalized

sources.title: US State Department International Religious Freedom Report - Panama 2023
Pew Research Center Religious Composition - Panama

countryOfOrigin.summary: Panama has one of the most retiree-friendly immigration systems in the world. The Pensionado visa offers significant discounts on healthcare, entertainment, travel, and more. The US dollar is legal tender, eliminating currency risk. Panama's economy has long been oriented toward international commerce (Canal, Colon Free Zone, banking). Americans are generally very well-received — the Canal Zone history means Panamanians have over a century of experience with American residents.
countryOfOrigin.legalProtections: Pensionado visa (Decree Law 6 of 1987) provides robust legal status, Law on Immigration and Naturalization, Friendly Nations visa for 50+ countries
positiveFactors: Pensionado visa is one of world's best retiree programs — extensive discounts and benefits
US dollar as legal tender eliminates currency exchange issues
Over a century of American-Panamanian coexistence via Canal Zone
Friendly Nations visa makes legal residency accessible
Large international community — Venezuelan, Colombian, American, Chinese, Indian
Banking and business infrastructure designed for international clients
riskFactors: Some resentment of Canal Zone history and US intervention legacy
Cost of living in Panama City has risen significantly — reducing retiree purchasing power advantage
Venezuelan immigration wave has created some anti-immigrant sentiment generally
Bureaucratic processes can be slow and require patience (or a paid lawyer)
Some discrimination against Haitian and other Caribbean immigrants

language.summary: Spanish is the official language and essential for daily life. However, Panama City is one of the more English-friendly cities in Latin America due to the Canal Zone legacy, international banking sector, and tourism industry. Many professionals in the city are bilingual. English signage is common in expat-heavy neighborhoods (Clayton, Costa del Este, Casco Viejo). Government services are in Spanish only. Healthcare providers in private hospitals often speak English.
language.legalProtections: Spanish is the sole official language. Indigenous languages have limited regional recognition under Law 88 of 2010.
positiveFactors: Canal Zone legacy means more bilingual professionals than most Latin American cities
Private healthcare (Punta Pacifica, Hospital Nacional) has English-speaking staff
International banking sector operates in English
Many real estate and legal services cater to English-speaking clients
Growing number of bilingual government forms for immigration processes
riskFactors: Government services officially in Spanish only
Outside Panama City, English proficiency drops dramatically
Social integration requires Spanish — retiree bubble is comfortable but isolating
Legal documents and contracts are in Spanish and legally binding in that language only

sources.title: US Embassy Panama - Living in Panama

expat_experience.communitySupport: Panama City has a large, established American expat community. Americans in Panama groups organize regular social events. The American Society of Panama has been active for decades. Coronado (beach community 1.5hr from city) has a particularly large retiree community. International churches, clubs, and volunteer organizations provide social infrastructure.
integrationResources: Servicio Nacional de Migracion — immigration services
Americans in Panama Facebook group (~20,000 members)
City of Knowledge (Ciudad del Saber) — international community hub
expat_experience.socialMediaSentiment: Mixed but generally positive. Cost of living concerns have increased significantly since 2023. Healthcare quality praised for private sector but public system criticized. Pensionado benefits widely lauded. Crime concerns in certain neighborhoods noted. Black American expats report generally positive experiences but note colorism. Language barrier is a common theme — those who learn Spanish report much better quality of life.
